coredata.dc:description: Photothermal therapy combined with chemotherapy has shown great promise in the treatment of cancer. In this synergistic system, a safe, stable, and efficient photothermal agent is desired. Herein, an effective photothermal agent, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), was initially synthesized and then rationally constructed a folic acid (FA)-targeted photothermal multifunctional nanoplatform by encapsulating CQDs and the anticancer drug doxorubicin (DOX) in the liposomes. Indocyanine green (ICG), a near infrared (NIR) photothermal agent, approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration, was embedded in the bilayer membrane to further enhance the photothermal effects and facilitate the rapid cleavage of liposomes for drug release. Triggered by the NIR laser, this engineered photothermal multifunctional nanoplatform, not only exhibited an excellent performance with the photothermal conversion efficiency of up to 47.14%, but also achieved controlled release of the payloads. In vitro, and in vivo experiments demonstrated that the photothermal multifunctional nanoplatform had excellent biocompatibility, enhanced tumor-specific targeting, stimuli-responsive drug release, effective cancer cell killing and tumor suppression through multi-modal synergistic therapy. The successful construction of this NIR light-triggered targeted photothermal multifunctional nanoplatform will provide a promising strategy for the design and development of synergistic chemo-photothermal combination therapy and improve the therapeutic efficacy of cancer treatment.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA. (a) TEM image of CQDs with inset of size distribution and HRTEM image. (b) TEM images of CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA. (c) TEM images of CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA after laser irradiation for 6 min. (d) TEM-EDS mapping image of CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA. (e) Hydrodynamic size of CQDs and CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA. (f) UV−Vis absorption spectra of CQDs, free ICG, free DOX, and CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA. (g) Size stability of CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA stored at 4 °C over a period of 7 days. (h) DOX release from CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA as a function of temperature. (i) Time course of DOX release from CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA at 37 °C and 42 °C.

Photothermal effect of CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA. (a) Heating curves of CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA at different concentrations under NIR laser irradiation (808 nm, 2 W/cm2) for 6 min. (b) Temperature variation of CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA solution (100 μg/mL) upon NIR laser irradiation and shut off (red line). And Plot of time vs −ln (θ) obtained from the cooling period (green line). (c) Temperature curves of CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA under five on/off cycles of NIR laser irradiation. (d, e) Digital thermal photograph of water and CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA under NIR laser irradiation at different time points. (f, g) Temperature distribution displayed in the white box of (d, e). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

In vitro cellular experiments. (a) Flow cytometry analysis of 4T1 cells cultured in different treatment groups. (b) Representative CLSM images of 4T1 cells incubated with different treatment groups. scale bar = 100 μm. (c) Fluorescent changes of DCFH activated in 4T1 cells incubated with CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA for different times after exposure to laser irradiation. scale bar = 200 μm. (d) Mean fluorescence intensity of cellular ROS generated by CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA under 808 nm laser irradiation for different times by flow cytometry analysis. (e, f) Relative cell viability of 4T1 cells incubated with different treatment groups at various concentrations with or without laser irradiation (808 nm, 2 W/cm2, 6 min) for 24 h. (g) Fluorescence images of 4T1 cells stained with calcein-AM (red, dead cells) and PI (green, live cells) after different treatments. scale bar = 200 μm, where *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

In vivo fluorescent and photothermal imaging. (a) Time-dependent fluorescence imaging of 4T1 tumor-bearing mice after injection with CQDs/ICG@LPs-FA and CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA. (b) Fluorescence imaging and (c) quantitative analysis of major organs and tumors after dissection. (d) The schematic diagram of tumor photothermal imaging. (e) Photothermal images and (f) heating curves of the tumor region in 4 T1 tumor-bearing mice during 808 nm laser irradiation.

In vivo anticancer activity and biosafety. (a) Schematic diagram of the therapeutic procedure in 4T1 tumor-bearing mice. (b) Tumor growth curves in different treatment groups. (c) Tumor weight and tumor photograph (d) in each group after 14 days of treatment. (e) Body weight change and tumor inhibition rate (f) of mice in different treatment groups. (g) H&E-stained image of tumor and main organs after different treatments. Scale bar 200 μm, where *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001.

(a) The CQD synthesis process. (b) Schematic diagram of the design and construction strategy of CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA. (c) Multi-modal synergistic antitumor therapy by CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA under near-infrared irradiation.

head.ce:title: Rational construction of CQDs-based targeted multifunctional nanoplatform for synergistic chemo-photothermal tumor therapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA combines photothermal therapy with chemotherapy in cancer treatment.

Photothermal therapy combined with chemotherapy has shown great promise in the treatment of cancer. In this synergistic system, a safe, stable, and efficient photothermal agent is desired. Herein, an effective photothermal agent, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), was initially synthesized and then rationally constructed a folic acid (FA)-targeted photothermal multifunctional nanoplatform by encapsulating CQDs and the anticancer drug doxorubicin (DOX) in the liposomes. Indocyanine green (ICG), a near infrared (NIR) photothermal agent, approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration, was embedded in the bilayer membrane to further enhance the photothermal effects and facilitate the rapid cleavage of liposomes for drug release. Triggered by the NIR laser, this engineered photothermal multifunctional nanoplatform, not only exhibited an excellent performance with the photothermal conversion efficiency of up to 47.14%, but also achieved controlled release of the payloads. In vitro, and in vivo experiments demonstrated that the photothermal multifunctional nanoplatform had excellent biocompatibility, enhanced tumor-specific targeting, stimuli-responsive drug release, effective cancer cell killing and tumor suppression through multi-modal synergistic therapy. The successful construction of this NIR light-triggered targeted photothermal multifunctional nanoplatform will provide a promising strategy for the design and development of synergistic chemo-photothermal combination therapy and improve the therapeutic efficacy of cancer treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The integration of multiple therapeutic modalities into a single nanoplatform to achieve synergistic treatment is recognized as a promising strategy to improve cancer therapy efficacy [1–4]. Among various synergistic strategies, photothermal therapy (PTT)-based combination therapy has shown excellent antitumor activity, especially the combination of photothermal therapy and chemotherapy [5–9]. This is due to the photothermal process that not only enables thermal ablation of the tumor, but also increases the permeability of cell membranes to facilitate drug uptake and helps to trigger tumor-specific drug release from drug-loaded nanoplatforms, which provides a continuous tumor cell killing to inhibit tumor recurrence and metastasis [10,11]. However, in this synergistic treatment strategy, a safe, stable and efficient photothermal agent is essential.

Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterial with sizes typically less than 10 nm, have gained great attention as an efficient photothermal agent in chemo-photothermal therapy due to their excellent biocompatibility, optical stability, negligible biological toxicity, facile preparation, and broadband absorption from ultraviolet to near-infrared [12–16]. For instance, Ardekani et al. synthesized a CQDs-DOX photoresponsive nanoparticle via charge interactions between the carboxyl/amine groups, which exhibited significantly higher cytotoxicity against breast cancer cells (MCF-7) through synergistic chemo-photothermal therapy compared to free DOX [17]. In another type of study, researchers chelated CQDs and chemotherapeutic drugs into larger nanoparticles, such as liposomes, mesoporous silica nanoparticles, mesoporous carbon nanoparticles or polydopamine nanoparticles [18–21]. Under near-infrared (NIR) light irradiation, these synergistic systems not only exhibited fascinating photothermal therapy performance, but also achieve controlled release of payload. However, these photothermal multifunctional nanoplatforms based on CQDs lack efficient tumor targeting specificity. Meanwhile, nanoscale CQDs have been reported to suffer from a rapid renal clearance and biodegradation, decreasing about 15 times within 1 h after intravenous injection [22]. Therefore, direct binding of drugs to CQDs has a short in vivo circulation time and cannot be efficiently enriched in tumor tissues, while chelating CQDs and drugs into large nanoparticles prolongs the in vivo circulation time, but the formed complexes are difficult to be biodegraded [23,24]. Thus, the development of a highly targeted, long in vivo circulation time, and biodegradable photothermal multifunctional nanoplatform based on CQDs is desirable.

Liposomes, spherical vesicles of phospholipids, are one of the most effective nanocarriers for drug delivery due to their excellent biocompatibility, biodegradability, low toxicity, prolonged half-life, and good solubility [25,26]. Meanwhile, liposomes can mimic the cell membrane in terms of structure and composition, and their surface can be modified for specific targeting [27]. Furthermore, our previous report demonstrated that ultrasmall nanoparticles can be controllably encapsulated into liposomes by regulating factors such as pressure, pH, and concentration [28]. These advantages make liposomes an ideal carrier for constructing a GQD-based photothermal multifunctional nanoplatform. Additionally, to further enhance photothermal effects and achieved the rapid cleavage of liposomes to release encapsulated drugs, indocyanine green (ICG), an NIR photothermal agent, approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration [29,30], is embedded in the liposome bilayer. This is due to the fact that ICG can generate heat and reactive oxygen species (ROS) upon NIR laser irradiation [31].

Inspired by this design idea, in this study, we first synthesized CQDs using a solvothermal method, and then rationally constructed a folic acid (FA)-targeted photothermal multifunctional nanoplatform by encapsulating CQDs and the anticancer drug DOX into the liposomes core and the ICG into the bilayer membrane. The engineered photothermal multifunctional nanoplatform not only exhibited negligible cytotoxicity, enhanced tumor-specific accumulation, controlled stimuli-responsive drug release and excellent photothermal performance, but also could significantly suppress tumor growth in 4T1 tumor-bearing mice by multi-modal synergistic antitumor therapy under 808 nm laser irradiation. Therefore, we believe that the successful construction of this targeted photothermal multifunctional nanoplatform will open new ways for combining photothermal therapy with chemotherapy to treat cancer.

1,2-Dipalmitoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine (DPPC), cholesterol and N-(carbonyl-methoxypolyethy-leneglycol 2000)-1, 2-distearoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphoethanolamine, sodium salt (DSPE-mPEG2000), were purchased from A.V.T Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). 1, 2-distearoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphoethanolamine-N-[(polyethylene glycol) 2000]-folate (DSPE-PEG2000-FA) were bought from Ponsure Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Sephadex G-150 was purchased from Sanjie Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX·HCl) was obtained from Maclin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). ICG, urea, citric acid, Dulbecco modified Eagle medium (DMEM), Chloroform (purity > 99 %), anhydrous methanol (purity > 99 %), and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, purity > 99 %) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm−1) was used in the experiments. All chemicals and reagents were of analytical grade and can be used without further purification.

CQDs were synthesized by the solvothermal method [20]. Briefly, urea (6.0 g) and citric acid (2.0 g) were dissolved in 30 mL DMSO. The solution was then transferred to a hydrothermal autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 4 h. After cooling the solution to room temperature, the insoluble solid impurities were removed by centrifugation at 15,000 rpm for 20 min, and then double the volume of ethanol was introduced to facilitate the precipitation of CQDs. Finally, the precipitate was collected and freeze-dried to a solid powder.

CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA were prepared by using a lipid film hydration and extrusion technology. Briefly, DPPC, cholesterol, DSPE-mPEG2000, DSPE-PEG2000-FA, and ICG were dissolved in chloroform and methanol (3:1, v/v) in a molar ratio of 25:5:1.5:1:1.2, and then evaporated through a rotary evaporator (SHZ-III, Shanghai Yarong Biochemical Instrument Factory, China) at 50 °C to remove the organic solvents. The resulting lipid film was hydrated with 250 mM ammonium sulfate solution containing CQDs at 50 °C for 1 h. The suspended solution was then extruded through a pore size of 400 and 100 nm polycarbonate membranes using a mini extruder (Avanti Polar Lipids Inc, USA) for 30 times. Unencapsulated CQDs and ICG were removed by gel filtration using a Sephadex G-150 column at a flow rate of 5 mL/min. DOX was encapsulated into the hydrophilic core of the CQDs/ICG@LPs-FA nanoparticles by the ammonium sulfate gradient method. The obtained CQDs/ICG@LPs-FA solution was placed in a dialysis bag (MWCO: 3,500 Da) and dialyzed for 12h to remove the free ammonium sulfate. The resulting solution and the DOX solution were mixed and then incubated for 2 h at 40 °C. The mixture was dialyzed to remove the free DOX. Finally, the obtained CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA were stored at 4 °C for further applications.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using a Bruker D8 Advance (Germany) under Cu Ka radiation. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were conducted on a ESCALAB Xi + XPS instrument (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) equipped with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source operating at 100 W. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained with a Perkin–Elmer spectrometer (USA). The fluoresce spectra of CQDs were measured by a FS5 instrument (Edinburgh, UK). The hydrodynamic diameter of CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA was measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS, ZetaSizer 3000HSA, Malvern Company, UK). The morphological study was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi HT7700, Tokyo, Japan) and high-resolution TEM (JEOL JEM-F200, Tokyo, Japan), respectively. The TEM-energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) elemental mapping was also performed using a JEOL JEM-F200 with a JED-2300 T energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer. UV−Vis − NIR absorption was measured by using a UV−Vis spectrophotometer (LAMB DA750, Waltham, MA). The temperature change of the solution was recorded using an infrared thermal imager (Fotric, 225 s-L24, USA).

where, φx represents the fluorescence QY, X is the CQDs. std is the Quinine Sulfate, which was chosen as the reference compound (φstd = 0.54 in 0.1 M H2SO4). A represents the optical absorption at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm. I represents the integrated fluorescence intensity over the fluorescence emission spectrum. η is the refractive index, which is equal to 1.33 for aqueous solution.
To reduce the influence of the reabsorption effect, the absorbance values were adjusted between 0 and 0.1 at 350 nm.

The encapsulation efficiencies (EE) of CQDs, ICG, and DOX in liposomes were determined using a UV−Vis spectrometer at λmax of 600, 480, and 780 nm, respectively, and the EE was calculated according to the following equation (2).

The photothermally triggered DOX release capability of CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA was studied by using the dialysis method. Briefly, 3 mL of CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA solution was loaded into a dialysis bag (MWCO: 1,000 Da) and dialyzed in 20 mL of phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.4) at different temperatures (37 °C and 42 °C) with continuous shaking at 200 rpm in the dark atmosphere. At predetermined time intervals, 1 mL of samples, was taken out and replaced with 1 mL of fresh phosphate buffer solution (PBS). The quantitation of DOX in the dialysate was determined by UV−Vis spectrophotometer at λmax of 480 nm. In addition, the release efficiency of DOX, from CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA at different temperatures (35, 37, 39, 41, 42, 43, 45, 47 °C) in 4 h, was obtained by using the same method described above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal performance of CQDs, ICG, and CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the photothermal performance, CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA containing different concentrations of CQDs (12.5, 25, 50, 100 μg/mL) were irradiated with an 808 nm laser at 2 W/cm2 for 6 min by a NIR laser device (LR-ISP-808/2000 mW, Changchun Lei Rui Optoelectronics Technology Co., Ltd. China). An infrared thermal imaging camera (Fotric 225 s-L24 USA) was used to record the temperature change of the solution at different time points. The photothermal effect of CQDs and free ICG at the same concentration was also investigated within 6 min, respectively. To evaluate the photothermal stability, 1 mL of CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA solution (100 μg/mL) was transferred to a quartz cuvette and exposed to an 808 nm laser (2 W/cm2) for five laser on/off cycles. To study the photothermal conversion efficiency (η), CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA at a concentration of 100 μg/mL were irradiated by NIR laser for 6 min and cooled for 10 min. Then, η was determined according to the equation described in previous literature [32,33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Targeting ability analysis and cellular uptake mechanism of CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA
ce:section.ce:para: For FA receptor-mediated target uptake, 4T1 cells at a density of 5 × 103 cells were implanted into confocal dishes and grown overnight. The old culture medium was then removed, and fresh medium containing CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA and CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs was added (PBS was used as control medium during the experiments). After incubation for 4 h, the cells were washed three-times with PBS solution and the fluorescence (FL) of DOX (λex = 488 nm, λem = 580 nm) was detected by confocal microscope (Olympus, FV-1000, Japan). For quantitative analysis of FL intensity, a flow cytometry (Beckman, USA) was used, and the operation was performed in the same manner as above reported.

To further investigate the cellular uptake mechanisms of CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA, 4T1 cells were seeded in 12-well plates at a density of 2 × 105 cells per well. After 12 h of incubation, the cells were pretreated with specific inhibitors at 37 °C or cultured at 4 °C for 30 min. Subsequently, they were incubated with CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA at 4 °C or in the presence of specific inhibitors at 37 °C for an additional 2 h. The concentrations of inhibitors were: 0.3 M sucrose, 20 μM colchicine, 25 μM chlorpromazine, 150 μM genistein, and 5 mM methyl-β-cyclodextrin. The cellular uptake of CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA without any treatment was designated as control. The flow cytometry was used to detect cellular uptake.

2′,7′-dichlorofuorescin diacetate (DCFH-DA, Beyotime, China) was used to detect intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) levels. First, 4T1 cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 5 × 103 cells per well. After 24 h incubation, the medium of each well was replaced with 1 mL of fresh medium containing CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA and further incubated for another 4 h. After washing several times with PBS, the cells were cultured with 1 mL of DCFH-DA (25 μM) for 15 min. Each well was then washed three times with PBS and irradiated with an NIR laser at 2 W/cm2 for different periods of time. Next, the cells were fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde solution for 10 min. Finally, the cells were washed several times with PBS, and the fluorescence (FL) of DCF (λex = 488, λem = 525 nm) was immediately captured by confocal microscope. Flow cytometry was used for quantitative analysis of FL intensity, and the operation was performed in the same manner as above reported.

The 4T1 cells were pre-seeded at a density of 5 × 103 cells per well in a 96-well plate and incubated for 24 h. Then, the cells were treated with fresh culture medium containing various concentrations of CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA, CQDs/ICG@LPs-FA and DOX for another 24 h, respectively. After 4 h of incubation, the NIR irradiation experimental groups were exposed to an NIR laser at a power density of 2 W/cm2 for 6 min, followed by incubation for another 20 h. All the cells were washed twice with PBS solution, and the cell viability of each group was analyzed using a cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8, MeilunBio, China). The absorbance of each well was determined at a wavelength of 450 nm using a microplate reader (Varioskan Flash, Thermo, USA). The combination index (CI) was calculated according to the equation reported in the literature [34]: CI=[IC50(combined therapy)/IC50(chemotherapy alone)] + [IC50(combined therapy)/IC50(PTT alone)], where CI values < 1, = 1 and > 1 indicate synergism, additive effects and antagonism, respectively.

For live and dead cell staining, 4T1 cells were cultured in a 96-well plate (5 × 103 cells per well) and incubated for 24 h. Then, CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA, CQDs/ICG@LPs-FA and DOX were added and cultured with the cells for 4 h. After that, the NIR irradiation groups were irradiated with an 808 nm NIR laser (2 W/cm2) for 6 min. Then, all experimental groups were washed twice with PBS solution, and the cells were co-stained with Calcein-AM/propidium iodide (PI) (Beyotime, China) for 30 min. Finally, the excess fluorescent dye was washed three times with PBS, and imaged using an inverted microscope (Leica, Germany).

All animal experiments were conducted in strict accordance with national guidelines for the protection and use of laboratory animals and approved by the Ethics Committee of Fudan University. To establish the breast cancer model, approximately 1 × 106 4T1 cells in 100 μL of serum-free medium were injected into the ventral mammary fat pad of BALB/c mice (female, 6 – 8 weeks). The tumor size was measured with a caliper and the volume was calculated as (V) = (tumor length) × (tumor width)2/2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo biodistribution and photothermal imaging
ce:section.ce:para: To study the in vivo distribution of CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA, 4T1 tumor-bearing mice were intravenously injected with CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA. At predesigned time intervals (2, 6, 12, 24, and 48 h), the 4T1 tumor-bearing mice were imaged using the IVIS Spectrum in vivo imaging system (Caliper PerkinElmer, Waltham, MA, USA). And then the mice were sacrificed to observe the fluorescence of heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney and tumor.

For in vivo photothermal imaging, 4T1 tumor-bearing mice were intravenously injected with 200 μL of PBS (control), CQDs/ICG@LPs-FA and CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA, respectively. After 12 h, an 808 nm laser (2 W/cm2) was used to irradiate the tumor region of the mice for 6 min. Meanwhile, an infrared thermal imaging camera was used to record the real-time thermal images and temperature change.

When the tumor volumes reached about 100 mm3, 4T1 tumor-bearing mice were randomly assigned to five groups: PBS (control group), PBS+Laser, free DOX, CQDs/ICG@LPs-FA+Laser and CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA+Laser (with equivalent loaded DOX dose at 3 mg/kg and CQDs at 7.5 mg/kg). The treatment was administered intravenously and the NIR irradiation experimental groups were exposed to an 808 nm laser at a power density of 2 W/cm2 for 6 min. Tumor volumes and body weight of mice were recorded every 2 days. After 14 days of treatment, all the mice were sacrificed, and their major organs and tumors were collected and stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) for further analysis.

Data were expressed as mean ± standard deviation (SD). Student’s t-test was used to evaluate the statistical significance. Significance was considered as *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, and ****p < 0.0001).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CQDs and CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs were initially synthesized using the solvothermal method as reported in our previous work (Scheme 1 a) [20]. The resulting CQDs had a broad absorption band ranging from the red to NIR region (Fig. 1 f), with a size distribution primarily between 1 and 4 nm (Fig. 1a). The high-resolution TEM image (inset of Fig. 1a) showed the circular shape of the CQDs, along with crystalline lattice fringes of 0.21 nm that corresponded to the (100) plane of graphene [35]. To further confirm the crystalline phase of the CQDs, XRD analysis was conducted. As shown in Fig. S1 a, a broad peak on the (002) crystal plane located at 27° was observed, which was consistent with previous reports [13,36]. The elemental compositions in CQDs were investigated by XPS, which showed the presence of C, N, O, and S with atomic ratios of 62.22 %, 11.65 %, 20.66 %, and 0.67 % (Fig. S1 b), respectively. The high-resolution C 1s spectra (Fig. S1 c) was deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.06 eV, 287.62 eV, and 288.47 eV, corresponding to the CC/CC, CN/CO, CS, and CO bonds, respectively. The N 1s spectra can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 399.35 eV, 400.02 eV, 401.78 eV, representing pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N, respectively (Fig. S1 d). The O 1s spectra (Fig. S1 e) contained two peaks at 531.1 and 533.02 eV, which suggest the presence of CO and C-OH/COC. For high-resolution S 2p spectra (Fig. S1 f), the band fitted three peaks at 164.05 eV, 166.3 eV, and 167.9 eV, which correspond to S 2p1/2, S 2p3/2, and SO. These results demonstrate CQDs have graphite cores and abundant −NH, −CH, and −CO surface groups. FT-IR spectra were further used to evaluate the surface functional groups. As shown in Fig. S1 g, the stretching vibrations related to −NH, −CH, and −CO were observed at 3174 cm−1, 2832 cm−1, and 1603 cm−1 respectively. Moreover, fluorescence spectra and photoluminescence emission spectra were examined to study the optical properties of CQDs. As displayed in Fig. S1 h and Fig. S1 i, CQDs exhibited an excitation wavelength-dependent behavior with a maximum excitation at 420 nm and a maximum emission at 530 nm. The quantum yield of CQDs was determined to be 18.6 % using Quinine Sulfate as the reference. Subsequently, we designed a folic acid (FA)-targeted photothermal multifunctional nanoplatform, termed as CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA (Scheme 1b). Briefly, a mixture of phospholipid components and NIR fluorescent dye ICG were co-dissolved in the organic phase and CQDs were dissolved in the aqueous phase. Next, CQDs was encapsulated in the liposome core and the ICG was intercalated into the bilayer membrane by using the thin film hydration procedure. After that, small molecule anticancer drug DOX was loaded using an ammonium sulfate gradient method. Upon 808 nm laser irradiation, CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA will heat up and generate ROS due to the preloaded CQDs and ICG, resulting in excellent photothermal effects and rapid cleavage of CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA to release the payloads, thereby achieving multimodal combined anticancer therapy (Scheme 1c).

The morphology and structure of CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA were carried out by TEM, demonstrating the synthesis of uniformly dispersed spherical particles with a small size (approximately 100 nm, Fig. 1b). This result endorsed the data obtained from DLS analysis (Fig. 1e). Such a size range is desirable that can passively penetrate into deeper tissues and tumor regions through the enhanced permeability and retention effect (EPR) [37,38]. Significantly, a large quantity of CQDs was observed to be trapped and dispersed within the hydrophilic core of the liposomes. The TEM-EDS mapping (Fig. 1d) also corroborated this observation, as the unique S elements in CQDs were clearly distributed on the surface of the engineered photothermal multifunctional nanoparticle. Fig. 1c showed the rupture of the liposome membrane and the release of payloads upon Near-Infrared (NIR) irradiation. UV−Vis spectra demonstrated that the obtained CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA had a strong absorption at around 800 nm and 480 nm (Fig. 1f), corresponding to the distinct absorption peaks for ICG and DOX, thereby confirming that ICG and DOX were successfully loaded. According to equation (1), the encapsulation efficiencies of CQDs, ICG, and DOX were calculated to be 75.58 %, 91.90 % and 90.55 %, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and DOX release characteristics in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the stability of obtained CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA, we stored them in a refrigerator at 4° C and monitored changes in particle size over a week. As shown in Fig. 1g and Figure S2 , the particle sizes of CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA remained around the initial size without aggregation and precipitation, indicating a good stability. The photothermally triggered DOX release ability of CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA was also studied by the dialysis method. The temperature of the CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA solution was set from 35 °C to 47 °C in two-unit increments. As shown in Fig. 1h, up to 80 % of DOX was released from the lipid nanohybrid system when the temperature is over 41 °C, while the release was less than 20 % at 39 °C and 37 ℃. This is because the main component of liposomes is DPPC, which has a phase transition temperature of 41.4 °C (Tm). By exceeding this temperature will cause the liposomes to modify transition from gel-sol to liquid–crystal phase and release encapsulated drugs [39]. Additionally, we further detected the real-time release percentage at 37 °C and 42 °C. As shown in Fig. 1i, the CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA showed good structural stability at 37 °C (T<Tm) with a relatively slow release of DOX after 4 h of incubation. In contrast, when the temperature was raised to 42 °C (T>Tm), there was an initial burst release over the first 10 min, followed by a diffusion-controlled slow release. Consequently, this engineered photothermal multifunctional nanoplatform can achieve on-demand release at the target site under the stimulation of temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Targeting ability analysis and cellular uptake mechanism of CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA
ce:section.ce:para:
Enhanced cellular internalization and thus increased intracellular photothermal agent concentration is essential to kill tumor cells. Here, 4T1 cells were used to study whether FA-modified nanohybrids could increase the cellular uptake. Specifically, two formulations (CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA, CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs) with the same DOX concentration were incubated with 4T1 cells for 4 h. As shown in Fig. 3 b, the cellular fluorescence intensity of CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA was much stronger than that of CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs (PBS medium was used as a control). Quantitative analysis ( Figure S5 ) demonstrated that the DOX fluorescence intensity of CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA was about 1.29 times higher than that of CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs in 4T1 cells. Additionally, flow cytometry analysis of DOX fluorescence signals showed that CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA had higher internalization in 4T1 cells than CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs (Fig. 3a and Figure S6
a). These results indicate that FA-modified nanoplatform can enhance targeted delivery to tumor cells with FA overexpression, which are consistent with previous reports that ligand-modified nanoparticles can increase intracellular accumulation via receptor-mediated endocytosis [44,45]. Moreover, the cellular uptake mechanisms of CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA were further investigated at different temperatures and in the presence of specific inhibitors. As shown in Figure S7 , low temperatures (4 °C) and hypertonic sucrose significantly suppressed cellular uptake compared to control group, whereas the presence of colchicine, chlorpromazine, genistein and methyl-β-cyclodextrin did not affect the cellular uptake, suggesting that the CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA was internalized via energy-dependent clathrin-mediated endocytosis.

The ROS, typically 1O2, were detected in 4T1 cells using DCFH-DA as a fluorescent indicator. This non-fluorescent molecule can be hydrolyzed by intracellular esterase to produce DCFH, and the resulting DCFH can be oxidized by intracellular 1O2 to produce fluorescent DCF, which makes living cells show green fluorescence. Specifically, 4T1 cells were incubated with CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA and then irradiated with a NIR laser (808 nm, 2 W/cm2) for different times. As shown in Fig. 3c, 4T1 cells displayed bright green fluorescence after laser irradiation, with fluorescence intensity markedly escalating as irradiation time extended. This result was also consistent with quantitative analysis by flow cytometry (Fig. 3d and Figure S6
b), where the DCF fluorescence intensity in 4T1 cells irradiated by laser for 6 min was 5.73-fold and 2.44-fold higher than those irradiated for 0 min and 3 min, respectively. The generation of ROS in CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA not only oxidizes liposomes to accelerate the cleavage of CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA to release encapsulated drugs, but also synergistically enhances the cytotoxicity of chemo-photothermal therapy [31,46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular cytotoxicity analysis and in vitro therapeutic effects
ce:section.ce:para: The cellular cytotoxicity of non-laser-irradiated CQDs, CQDs/ICG@LPs-FA and CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA was assessed using the CCK-8 assay. As shown in Fig. 3e and Figure S8 , the cell viability in these groups remained above 85 % for all concentrations tested, thus demonstrating a good biocompatibility of hybrid nanosystems. For in vitro therapeutic experiments, the 4T1 cells were incubated with different treatment groups, and then the CQDs/ICG@LPs-FA group and CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA group were irradiated with a NIR laser (808 nm, 2 W/cm2) for 6 min. As shown in Fig. 3f, the cell viability of each groups displayed a concentration-dependent cellular cytotoxicity. Notably, the CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA+laser group exhibited enhanced therapeutic effects compared to the DOX group and the CQDs/ICG/@LPs-FA+laser group. This was because CQDs/ICC/DOX@LPs-FA not only generated heat and abundant 1O2 upon NIR laser stimulation but also released the entrapped chemotherapeutic drug DOX, thus achieving a synergistic therapy effect. Furthermore, the combination index (CI), a characteristic coefficient used to evaluate the synergistic efficacy of the combination therapy, was calculated to be 0.822, which is less than 1. This result demonstrated that the chemo-photothermal combination therapy based on CQDs/ICC/DOX@LPs-FA exhibited an efficient synergistic therapeutic effect in tumor.

To further validate the therapeutic effects of these groups in vitro, the live/dead staining experiments were carried out. 4T1 cells that received different treatments were co-stained with calcein-AM (green, live cells) and PI (red, dead cells) and observed using an inverted microscope. As shown in Fig. 3g and Figure S9 , there are no apparent cell viability differences in control (PBS), laser group, CQDs/ICG@LPs-FA group and CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA group, indicating that laser and nanoplatform alone did not cause cytotoxic death. However, most of the cells in the DOX group, CQDs/ICG/@LPs-FA+laser group and CQDs/ICG/@LPs-FA+laser group were dead, with the CQDs/ICG/@LPs-FA+laser group showing enhanced cytotoxic effects. These results were also consistent with those of the CCK-8 assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo biodistribution and photothermal imaging
ce:section.ce:para: To study the biodistribution of photothermal multifunctional nanoplatform in vivo, CQDs/ICG@LPs-FA or CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA, at an equivalent CQDs concentration, were intravenously injected into the 4T1 tumor-bearing mice, and the fluorescence of CQDs was detected at different time intervals using the IVIS Spectrum fluorescence imaging system. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the fluorescence signals in the tumor region were gradually increased and reached a peak at 12 h post-injection, showing the nanoplatform’s systemic circulation and tumor-targeted accumulation process. This is because nanoparticles with approximately 100 nm can reach the tumor region through enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effects, as well as FA-modification can further facilitate specific targeted delivery to tumor cells [38,45]. Subsequently, with elapsing of time, the fluorescence signals of CQDs in the tumor region became weaker and the signals from other parts of the body were quite low. After 48 h, mice were euthanized to collect the main organs and tumor for ex vivo fluorescence imaging. As shown in Fig. 4b, the fluorescence signals in tumor were stronger than those in heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney, aligning with quantitative analysis in Fig. 4c. Notably, kidney fluorescence was high due to renal filtration of GQDs (<10 nm) in vivo.

Encouraged by the substantial accumulation of the nanoplatform in the tumor, in vivo photothermal capacity was further evaluated. 4T1 tumor-bearing mice that accepted an intravenous injection of PBS, CQDs/ICG@LPs-FA or CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA were irradiated with the NIR laser (2 W/cm2) for 6 min at 12 h post-injection. The temperature variation of tumor areas was recorded using an infrared thermal imaging camera (Fig. 4d). The results showed that both CQDs/ICG@LPs-FA+Laser and CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA+Laser had a significant temperature increase in the tumor region (11.1 °C and 11.3 °C, respectively), which was high enough to induce irreversible apoptosis of deep-seated malignant cells through hyperthermia (Fig. 4e and 4f). In contrast, mice treated with PBS+Laser, a slight temperature rise (3.3 °C) was detected in the tumor area. These results demonstrated the excellent photothermal performance and thermal imaging capability of the nanoplatform in vivo.

Following encouraging results from in vitro studies and promising in vivo photothermal performance and tumor accumulation, we proceeded to investigate the in vivo antitumor efficacy of CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA in 4T1 tumor-bearing mice. The experimental protocol is outlined in Fig. 5 a, where mice were randomly assigned to five groups receiving PBS (control), PBS, DOX, CQDs/ICG@LPs-FA, and CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA treatments, respectively. Then, the NIR irradiation experimental groups were exposed to an 808 nm laser at a power density of 2 W/cm2 for 6 min at 12 h post injection. As shown in Fig. 5b, the tumor volumes of the mice treated with PBS (control) and PBS+Laser had a rapid tumor growth over the course of therapy, indicating NIR irradiation alone could not inhibit the growth of tumors. Mice receiving free DOX showed mild tumor growth during the assay period, attributed to limited drug accumulation in the tumor region and poor chemotherapeutic response. Similarly, mice treated with CQDs/ICG@LPs-FA+NIR also showed moderate inhibition of tumor growth. Therefore, neither chemotherapy nor photothermal therapy proved effective in curbing tumor growth. In stark contrast, mice treated with CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA+Laser showed significantly inhibited tumor growth, achieving a tumor inhibition rate of up to 85.6 % (Fig. 5f). This preferable therapeutic outcome may be attributed to the synergistic treatment effects of CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA+Laser, where the nanoplatform significantly accumulate at the tumor site. Subsequently, upon exposure to 808 nm laser irradiation, these nanoplatform were heated and rapid cleavage to release the encapsulated chemotherapeutic drug DOX, which achieve a multi-modal synergistic antitumor therapy. Furthermore, the tumor photographs and tumor weight showed consistent trends among the different treatment groups, with the CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA+Laser group which have the smallest tumor size and tumor weight (Fig. 5c and 5d).

To further evaluate the therapeutic effect, tumors from different groups were sectioned and H&E stained. As shown in Fig. 5g, the CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA+Laser group presented more severe tumor cells necrosis and morphological changes compared to the other treatment groups. In contrast, the tumor cells in the PBS group and PBS+Laser group retained the normal morphology and tight packing. Simultaneously, in vivo biosafety was also preliminarily studied. As shown in Fig. 5e and 5g, there were no significant variations in body weights or pathological abnormalities of the main organs among the different treatment groups, indicating that CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA has good biological safety and could be an ideal drug carrier for photothermal anticancer therapy.

In this study, we rationally constructed an NIR light-triggered targeted photothermal multifunctional nanoplatform (CQDs/ICG/DOX@LPs-FA), which consisted of an FA-modified liposome that loaded photothermal CQDs and the anticancer drug DOX in the liposome core and ICG in the bilayer membrane. Upon the NIR light irradiation, the engineered nanoplatform not only achieved controlled drug release, but also exhibited a good photothermal performance. This engineered nanoplatform had negligible cytotoxicity and enhanced tumor-specific accumulation via FA-mediated endocytosis. In vivo anticancer efficacy demonstrated that this engineered nanoplatform could significantly inhibit tumor volume growth in 4T1 tumor-bearing mice through the multi-modal synergistic antitumor therapy. Therefore, we believe that the successful construction of this targeted photothermal multifunctional nanoplatform will provide an insightful strategy for combining photothermal therapy with chemotherapy in cancer treatment.
